schools.summary: The heading and the divine image: 'this is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him' — the formula 'this is the book of the generations (toledoth) of Adam' introducing the genealogy of Adam through Seth's line to Noah (ch. 5), 'the book' suggesting a written record or register of the generations, the godly line of promise traced; 'in the likeness of God made he him' recalling the dignity of man's creation in God's image (1:26-27), the genealogy opening by reminding the reader that man, despite the fall and the death that runs through the chapter, was made in God's likeness, the original dignity set against the mortality that follows; man made in God's likeness, yet (v.3) Adam begetting a son in his own fallen likeness, the chapter holding together the divine image in which man was made and the death which sin brought, the image of God marking man's worth, the refrain 'and he died' marking the wages of sin — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Calvin, Gill, Henry.

themes: This is the book of the generations of Adam. In the day that God created man, in the likeness of God made he him
the heading of Adam's generations and man made in God's likeness
the line of promise traced from man made in God's image
the divine image set against the mortality of the chapter

schools.summary: Mankind created and named: 'male and female created he them; and blessed them, and called their name Adam, in the day when they were created' — God creating mankind as male and female (1:27, both sexes God's creation, the recapitulation of the creation of man recalling the original constitution of the race in the two sexes); 'blessed them' (the blessing of fruitfulness and dominion given to mankind at creation, 1:28, God's favor resting on the race he made, the blessing recalling God's original purpose for man); 'called their name Adam (man)' (the name 'Adam' denoting both the first man and mankind, the race, the man and the woman together called 'Adam,' for the woman is of the man and one with him, the name marking the unity of the race in its head, the same name, Adam, for the individual and for mankind) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: Male and female created he them; and blessed them, and called their name Adam
mankind created male and female, blessed, and named Adam
the race created in the two sexes and blessed by God
mankind united in the name of its head, Adam

schools.summary: Adam begets Seth in his own image: 'and Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in his own likeness, after his image; and called his name Seth' — the genealogy beginning with Adam begetting Seth at 130 years (after the loss of Abel and the line of Cain), the godly line continuing through Seth (4:25), the chapter tracing this line to Noah; 'in his own likeness, after his image' in pointed contrast to Adam himself, made in the likeness of God (v.1; 1:26) — Adam, now fallen, begetting a son in his own (now corrupt, mortal) likeness, the natural likeness of father to son including the transmission of the fallen, sinful nature (original sin propagated), Seth, like all Adam's offspring, inheriting the Adamic, fallen image, not the unfallen divine image in its integrity; 'called his name Seth (appointed)' (the appointed seed, 4:25, in whose line the promise continues, the godly line traced from Adam through Seth, the head of the line of promise) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Adam lived an hundred and thirty years, and begat a son in his own likeness, after his image; and called his name Seth
Adam begetting Seth in his own image
the transmission of the fallen Adamic likeness, in contrast to the divine image
the godly line of promise continuing through Seth

schools.summary: Adam's long life after Seth: 'and the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years: and he begat sons and daughters' — Adam living eight hundred years after Seth's birth (the great longevity of the antediluvians, Adam's long life allowing him to see many generations of his descendants, the patriarch living through the early history of the race); the long lifespans of the antediluvians (here, Adam's eight hundred years after Seth) taken as literal years (the great age of the patriarchs before the flood serving to people the earth rapidly and to preserve and transmit the knowledge of God and the promise through few generations, God's purpose in the long lives of the early fathers); 'he begat sons and daughters' (Adam having many other children besides Seth, and Cain and Abel, the race multiplying through his numerous offspring, the genealogy naming only the line of promise, Seth, but noting the many sons and daughters, the rapid increase of mankind) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: the days of Adam after he had begotten Seth were eight hundred years: and he begat sons and daughters
Adam's long life and many children after Seth
the great longevity of the antediluvians, taken as literal years
the rapid multiplying of the race

schools.summary: The death of Adam: 'and all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years: and he died' — the full span of Adam's life, nine hundred and thirty years (the great age of the first man, Adam living nearly a thousand years, witnessing the early generations of the race he fathered); Adam's long life, though great, coming to an end (the longest life finite, even Adam, who lived nigh a thousand years, dying at last, the length of life unable to exempt him from the sentence of death); 'and he died' the solemn refrain that tolls through the chapter (vv.8, 11, 14, etc., the sentence of death, 2:17; 3:19, 'dust thou art, and unto dust shalt thou return,' taking effect, despite the long lives every patriarch dying, 'and he died' the seal of the curse on sin repeated over each name, death reigning from Adam, Romans 5:14, the long lives ending in the grave, save Enoch, v.24) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: all the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years: and he died
the total of Adam's years and his death
the great life of the first man ending in the grave
the refrain 'and he died' the seal of the sentence on sin

schools.summary: Seth begets Enos: 'and Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos' — Seth, the head of the godly line (4:25-26), begetting Enos at 105 years (the line of promise continuing from Adam through Seth to Enos, in Enos's day men beginning to call on the name of the LORD, 4:26); Seth, the appointed seed, living and begetting in the line of promise (the godly line carefully traced, generation by generation, the faithfulness of God in preserving the line through which the promised seed would come); 'begat Enos' (Enos, whose name means 'man,' frail, mortal, the son of Seth, in whose time the public worship of God began, 4:26, the line of Seth, marked by the worship of the LORD, in contrast to the line of Cain, the genealogy of the godly carrying the promise forward, the continuance of the line of faith) — Gill, Henry.

themes: Seth lived an hundred and five years, and begat Enos
Seth begetting Enos, continuing the line
the godly line of promise carried from Seth to Enos
the line of faith marked by the worship of the LORD

schools.summary: Seth's long life after Enos: 'and Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters' — Seth, like the other antediluvian patriarchs, living to a great age (eight hundred and seven years after Enos) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the patriarchs); Seth's long life, like Adam's (v.4), part of the pattern of the antediluvians' great longevity (the patriarchs living for centuries, seeing many generations, the long lives serving to people the earth and preserve the line); 'begat sons and daughters' (Seth having many other children besides Enos, the race multiplying through the numerous offspring of the patriarchs, the genealogy naming the line of promise, Enos, but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind through the long-lived fathers) — Gill, JFB.

themes: Seth lived after he begat Enos eight hundred and seven years, and begat sons and daughters
Seth's long life and many children after Enos
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity and numerous offspring

schools.summary: The death of Seth: 'and all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: and he died' — Seth's full life, nine hundred and twelve years, a great age like the other antediluvians, and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the solemn toll that follows each patriarch (Seth, the godly head of the line of promise, dying like the rest); Seth living over nine centuries, among the longest-lived of the patriarchs, yet his long life, like all, coming to its end (the great age unable to avert death); 'and he died' the recurring seal of mortality (vv.5, 11, 14, etc., the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect on Seth as on Adam, even the godly patriarch of the line of promise dying, the refrain tolling through the chapter, marking that death reigns over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:12, 14, save Enoch, v.24, the long lives ending in the grave) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Seth were nine hundred and twelve years: and he died
the total of Seth's years and his death
the great life of the appointed seed ending in the grave
the refrain 'and he died' marking that death reigns over all

schools.summary: Enos begets Cainan: 'and Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan' — Enos, the son of Seth (in whose day men called on the LORD, 4:26), begetting Cainan at ninety years (the line of promise continuing from Seth through Enos to Cainan, the godly genealogy carried forward); Enos, the third from Adam in the godly line, living and begetting in the line of faith (the genealogy tracing the line carefully, God preserving the line of promise generation by generation); 'begat Cainan' (Cainan, a name distinct from Cain, the son of Enos, the next in the line of Seth, the line of the godly continuing toward Noah, the careful record of the generations marking God's faithfulness in preserving the seed through which the promise would come, the line of faith carried forward) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Enos lived ninety years, and begat Cainan
Enos begetting Cainan, continuing the line
the line of promise continuing from Seth through Enos to Cainan
God's faithfulness in preserving the line of faith

schools.summary: Enos's long life after Cainan: 'and Enos lived after he begat Cainan eight hundred and fifteen years, and begat sons and daughters' — Enos, like the other patriarchs, living to a great age (eight hundred and fifteen years after Cainan) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers); Enos's long life, like those before him, part of the antediluvian pattern of great longevity (the patriarchs living for centuries, the long lives, taken as literal, serving God's purpose to people the earth and preserve the line of promise); 'begat sons and daughters' (Enos having many other children besides Cainan, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' numerous offspring, the genealogy naming only the line of promise, Cainan, but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind) — Gill.

themes: Enos lived after he begat Cainan eight hundred and fifteen years, and begat sons and daughters
Enos's long life and many children after Cainan
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity

schools.summary: The death of Enos: 'and all the days of Enos were nine hundred and five years: and he died' — Enos's full life, nine hundred and five years, a great age, and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the recurring toll of mortality (Enos, the godly patriarch in whose day men called on the LORD, 4:26, dying like the rest); Enos living over nine centuries, like the patriarchs before him, yet his long life ending in death (the great age unable to exempt him from the sentence); 'and he died' the seal of mortality repeated over each name (vv.5, 8, 14, etc., the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect, even the godly Enos who called on the LORD dying, the refrain marking that death reigns over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:12, 14, save Enoch, v.24, the chapter a genealogy of death as well as of the line of promise) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Enos were nine hundred and five years: and he died
the total of Enos's years and his death
the great life of the patriarch ending in the grave
the refrain marking that death reigns over all

schools.summary: Cainan begets Mahalaleel: 'and Cainan lived seventy years, and begat Mahalaleel' — Cainan, the son of Enos, begetting Mahalaleel at seventy years (the line of promise continuing from Enos through Cainan to Mahalaleel, the godly genealogy carried forward); Cainan, the fourth from Adam in the godly line, living and begetting in the line of faith (the genealogy tracing the line carefully, generation by generation, God preserving the line of promise); 'begat Mahalaleel' (Mahalaleel, whose name means 'praise of God' or 'God is praised,' the son of Cainan, the next in the line of Seth, the line of the godly continuing toward Noah, the name fitting the godly line that called on the LORD, the line of faith carried forward) — Gill.

themes: Cainan lived seventy years, and begat Mahalaleel
Cainan begetting Mahalaleel, continuing the line
the line of promise continuing from Enos through Cainan to Mahalaleel
the godly genealogy carried forward, the name praising God

schools.summary: Cainan's long life after Mahalaleel: 'and Cainan lived after he begat Mahalaleel eight hundred and forty years, and begat sons and daughters' — Cainan, like the other patriarchs, living to a great age (eight hundred and forty years after Mahalaleel) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers); Cainan's long life part of the antediluvian pattern (the patriarchs living for centuries, the long lives serving to people the earth and to preserve and transmit the line and the knowledge of God through few generations); 'begat sons and daughters' (Cainan having many other children besides Mahalaleel, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' numerous offspring, the genealogy naming the line of promise but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind) — Gill, JFB.

themes: Cainan lived after he begat Mahalaleel eight hundred and forty years, and begat sons and daughters
Cainan's long life and many children after Mahalaleel
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity

schools.summary: The death of Cainan: 'and all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: and he died' — Cainan's full life, nine hundred and ten years, a great age, and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the recurring seal of mortality (Cainan, the godly patriarch of the line of promise, dying like the rest); Cainan living over nine centuries, yet his long life, like all, ending in death (the great age unable to avert the sentence); 'and he died' the toll of mortality repeated over each name (vv.5, 8, 11, etc., the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect, even the godly patriarch dying, death reigning from Adam over all his sons, Romans 5:14, save Enoch, v.24, the genealogy of the line of promise also a genealogy of death, the long lives ending in the grave, marking the curse on sin) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Cainan were nine hundred and ten years: and he died
the total of Cainan's years and his death
the great life ending in the grave
the refrain marking the curse on sin, death reigning from Adam

schools.summary: Mahalaleel begets Jared: 'and Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared' — Mahalaleel, the son of Cainan, begetting Jared at sixty-five years (the line of promise continuing from Cainan through Mahalaleel to Jared, the godly genealogy carried forward toward Noah); Mahalaleel (whose name means 'praise of God'), the fifth from Adam in the godly line, living and begetting in the line of faith (the genealogy tracing the line carefully, God preserving the line of promise generation by generation); 'begat Jared' (Jared, whose name may mean 'descent,' the son of Mahalaleel, the next in the line of Seth, the line of the godly continuing, in Jared's line would come Enoch, who walked with God, vv.18-24, the line of faith carried forward toward the great patriarchs) — Gill.

themes: Mahalaleel lived sixty and five years, and begat Jared
Mahalaleel begetting Jared, continuing the line
the line of promise continuing from Cainan through Mahalaleel to Jared
the godly genealogy carried forward toward Enoch and Noah

schools.summary: Mahalaleel's long life after Jared: 'and Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters' — Mahalaleel, like the other patriarchs, living to a great age (eight hundred and thirty years after Jared) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers); Mahalaleel's long life part of the antediluvian pattern (the patriarchs living for centuries, the long lives serving God's purpose to people the earth and preserve the line of promise through few generations); 'begat sons and daughters' (Mahalaleel having many other children besides Jared, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' offspring, the genealogy naming the line of promise but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind) — Gill.

themes: Mahalaleel lived after he begat Jared eight hundred and thirty years, and begat sons and daughters
Mahalaleel's long life and many children after Jared
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity

schools.summary: The death of Mahalaleel: 'and all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and five years: and he died' — Mahalaleel's full life, eight hundred ninety-five years, a great age, and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the recurring toll of mortality (Mahalaleel, the godly patriarch whose name praises God, dying like the rest); Mahalaleel living nearly nine centuries, yet his long life, like all, coming to its end (the great age unable to exempt him from death); 'and he died' the seal of mortality repeated over each name (vv.5, 8, 11, 14, the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect, even the godly patriarch who praised God dying, death reigning over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:12, 14, save Enoch, v.24, the long lives ending in the grave, the refrain marking the universal reign of death from the fall) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Mahalaleel were eight hundred ninety and five years: and he died
the total of Mahalaleel's years and his death
the great life ending in the grave
the refrain marking the universal reign of death from the fall

schools.summary: Jared begets Enoch: 'and Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch' — Jared, the son of Mahalaleel, begetting Enoch at one hundred sixty-two years (the line of promise continuing from Mahalaleel through Jared to Enoch, Enoch the notable patriarch who walked with God and was translated, vv.21-24); Jared, the sixth from Adam in the godly line, living and begetting in the line of faith (the genealogy tracing the line carefully, Jared's significance lying chiefly in his son Enoch, the seventh from Adam, Jude 14, the most eminent of the antediluvian patriarchs); 'he begat Enoch' (Enoch, a different Enoch from Cain's son, 4:17, the son of Jared, the seventh from Adam, the patriarch who walked with God and was taken without dying, vv.22-24, the genealogy, otherwise a record of death, coming to the one man who did not die, Jared's son Enoch the bright exception in the chapter) — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: Jared lived an hundred sixty and two years, and he begat Enoch
Jared begetting Enoch, the godly patriarch who walked with God
the line of promise continuing to Enoch, the seventh from Adam
the genealogy of death coming to the one who did not die

schools.summary: Jared's long life after Enoch: 'and Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and begat sons and daughters' — Jared, like the other patriarchs, living to a great age (eight hundred years after Enoch) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers); Jared's long life part of the antediluvian pattern (the patriarchs living for centuries, the long lives serving to people the earth and preserve the line, Jared living to see many generations of his descendants); 'begat sons and daughters' (Jared having many other children besides Enoch, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' offspring, the genealogy naming the line of promise but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind) — Gill, JFB.

themes: Jared lived after he begat Enoch eight hundred years, and begat sons and daughters
Jared's long life and many children after Enoch
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity

schools.summary: The death of Jared: 'and all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died' — Jared's full life, nine hundred sixty-two years, among the longest of the patriarchs, and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the recurring toll of mortality (Jared, the father of the godly Enoch, dying like the rest); Jared living over nine and a half centuries, one of the longest-lived of the antediluvians (second to Methuselah), yet his long life, like all, ending in death (the great age unable to avert the sentence); 'and he died' the seal of mortality repeated over each name (the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect, even Jared, the father of Enoch who did not die, dying himself, the contrast striking — Jared dies but his son Enoch is taken without death, v.24, death reigning over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:14, save the one exception, the long lives ending in the grave) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Jared were nine hundred sixty and two years: and he died
the total of Jared's years and his death
the great life ending in the grave, in contrast to his son Enoch
the refrain marking the reign of death save the one exception

schools.summary: Enoch begets Methuselah: 'and Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah' — Enoch, the seventh from Adam (Jude 14), begetting Methuselah at sixty-five years (the line of promise continuing from Jared through Enoch to Methuselah, Methuselah becoming the longest-lived of all men, v.27); Enoch, the godly patriarch who walked with God (vv.22, 24), beginning (as the text will show) to walk with God especially after the birth of Methuselah (the begetting of his son marking a turning in his godly walk, Enoch the most eminent of the antediluvians); 'begat Methuselah' (Methuselah, whose name is variously interpreted, some reading it 'when he dies, it shall be sent,' a possible reference to the flood, which came the year of Methuselah's death, the longest-lived man, 969 years, v.27, the line of promise continuing through Methuselah to Lamech and Noah, God's patience marked in the long life of Methuselah before the flood) — Gill, Henry.

themes: Enoch lived sixty and five years, and begat Methuselah
Enoch begetting Methuselah, the longest-lived patriarch
the line of promise continuing through the godly Enoch
the birth of Methuselah marking the deepening of Enoch's walk with God

schools.summary: Enoch walked with God: 'and Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years, and begat sons and daughters' — instead of the usual formula ('lived... years'), the text saying Enoch 'walked with God' (for three hundred years after Methuselah's birth, Enoch walking with God, the phrase marking a life of intimate fellowship with God, holiness, and obedience, Enoch's whole life a walk with God); to 'walk with God' is to live in close communion and agreement with him (Amos 3:3, 'can two walk together, except they be agreed?', a life of faith, holiness, and continual fellowship, Enoch pleasing God, Hebrews 11:5, believing and obeying him, his walk with God the pattern of the godly life amid an ungodly generation, Enoch also prophesying, Jude 14-15); 'begat sons and daughters' (Enoch, even in his close walk with God, living an ordinary family life, begetting children, the godly walk compatible with the duties and relations of life, holiness lived out in the family and the world) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: Enoch walked with God after he begat Methuselah three hundred years, and begat sons and daughters
Enoch walking with God for three hundred years
the life of intimate fellowship, faith, and holiness, pleasing God
the godly walk lived out amid an ungodly age and in family life

schools.summary: The days of Enoch: 'and all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years' — Enoch's life three hundred sixty-five years, notably shorter than the other antediluvians (who lived eight or nine centuries, Enoch's days comparatively few, for he was taken early, v.24); Enoch's three hundred sixty-five years, while a full life by later reckoning, short among the long-lived antediluvians, yet his life not cut short by death but by translation (v.24, God taking him in the midst of his years, not as a punishment but as a reward); unlike every other patriarch in the chapter (vv.5, 8, 11, etc.), the refrain 'and he died' not said of Enoch (the omission preparing for v.24, 'and he was not; for God took him,' Enoch did not die but was translated, the breaking of the chapter's death-refrain marking Enoch as the great exception, the man who walked with God not seeing death) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Enoch were three hundred sixty and five years
the total of Enoch's years and the absence of the death-refrain
Enoch's comparatively short life, taken early by translation
the striking omission of 'and he died,' marking the great exception

schools.summary: Enoch's translation: 'and Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him' — 'Enoch walked with God' (repeated from v.22) the summary of Enoch's whole life (a walk of faith, holiness, and intimate fellowship with God, the LXX rendering it 'Enoch pleased God,' the basis of Hebrews 11:5, 'by faith Enoch was translated... he pleased God,' Enoch's godliness set as the reason for his translation); Ellicott, Calvin, and Wesley hold that Enoch was translated — taken bodily to heaven without dying, as Elijah later was (2 Kings 2:11), 'he was not (found among men); for God took him (to himself),' God removing Enoch from the earth without death, Hebrews 11:5 plainly teaching this: 'Enoch was translated that he should not see death,' the translation a reward of his faith and a pledge of immortality, the man who walked with God not dying but taken to God; Enoch's translation, in an age of death and impending judgment, a testimony to the immortality of the soul and the hope of life beyond death, the bright exception in the genealogy of death — Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'God took him' mean — translation, or early death?

sides.position: Enoch was translated — taken bodily to heaven without dying, like Elijah; he did not see death (the dominant reading, Hebrews 11:5)
A euphemism for an early death — 'God took him' meaning Enoch died young
holders: the rationalist reading (noted and rejected by Ellicott)

themes: Enoch walked with God: and he was not; for God took him
Enoch's walk with God and his translation (the crux of 'God took him')
translated to heaven without death, like Elijah (Hebrews 11:5), vs a euphemism for early death
the bright exception in the genealogy of death, the pledge of immortality

schools.summary: Methuselah begets Lamech: 'and Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech' — Methuselah, the son of the translated Enoch, begetting Lamech at one hundred eighty-seven years (the line of promise continuing from Enoch through Methuselah to Lamech, the father of Noah, the godly genealogy carrying on toward the flood and Noah); Methuselah, the longest-lived of all men (969 years, v.27), the son of Enoch and the grandfather of Noah (his very long life spanning much of the antediluvian age, God's patience shown in the long life of Methuselah before the flood, which came in the year of his death); 'begat Lamech' (Lamech, a different Lamech from Cain's line, 4:18, the son of Methuselah, the father of Noah, the line of promise continuing, the godly Lamech, in contrast to the boastful Lamech of Cain's line, naming his son Noah in hope of comfort, v.29, the line of faith carried to Noah) — Gill, Henry.

themes: Methuselah lived an hundred eighty and seven years, and begat Lamech
Methuselah begetting Lamech, continuing the line
the line of promise continuing from Enoch through Methuselah to Lamech
the godly line carried to Noah's father

schools.summary: Methuselah's long life after Lamech: 'and Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters' — Methuselah, the longest-lived of men, living seven hundred eighty-two years after Lamech and begetting many children (his very long life, totaling 969 years, v.27, the greatest of all the antediluvians); Methuselah's life, the longest in Scripture, spanning the whole age from near Adam's time to the flood (God's patience and forbearance marked in the long life of Methuselah, whose death, in his 969th year, coincided with the coming of the flood, the long life a testimony to God's longsuffering before judgment); 'begat sons and daughters' (Methuselah having many other children besides Lamech, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' offspring, the genealogy naming the line of promise but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind before the flood) — Gill.

themes: Methuselah lived after he begat Lamech seven hundred eighty and two years, and begat sons and daughters
Methuselah's long life and many children after Lamech
the longest life of all men, marking God's patience before the flood
the increase of mankind before the flood

schools.summary: The death of Methuselah: 'and all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years: and he died' — Methuselah living nine hundred sixty-nine years, the longest life recorded in Scripture, and then the refrain, 'and he died' (the longest-lived of all men dying at last, even the greatest age ending in death); Methuselah's nine hundred sixty-nine years the greatest age of any man (his long life a monument to God's patience, notably, by the chronology, Methuselah dying in the very year of the flood, his name, on one reading, 'when he dies, it shall be sent,' pointing to the flood, God's longsuffering waiting through Methuselah's whole long life before sending the judgment); 'and he died' (even Methuselah, the longest-lived, dying, the refrain sealing the chapter's theme — death reigns over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:14, and the longest life is no escape, the great age only magnifying the certainty of death, the longest-lived patriarch, like all, ending in the grave, save Enoch, v.24) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: all the days of Methuselah were nine hundred sixty and nine years: and he died
the total of Methuselah's years - the longest life - and his death
the longest life of any man, a monument to God's patience
even the longest-lived dying, in the very year of the flood

schools.summary: Lamech begets Noah: 'and Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son' — Lamech, the son of Methuselah, begetting a son (Noah, named in v.29) at one hundred eighty-two years (the line of promise continuing from Methuselah through Lamech to Noah, the godly genealogy reaching its goal in Noah, through whom the race and the line would be preserved through the flood); Lamech, of the godly line of Seth (a different Lamech from Cain's boastful descendant, 4:18-24), living and begetting in the line of faith (the genealogy carrying the line to the threshold of Noah, the godly Lamech's hope, v.29, contrasting with the violent boast of Cain's Lamech); 'begat a son' (the son, not yet named in this verse, Noah, v.29, the tenth from Adam, through Noah the line of promise surviving the flood, Noah the bridge from the antediluvian world to the new world after the flood, the line of faith reaching its preserver in Noah) — Gill, Henry.

themes: Lamech lived an hundred eighty and two years, and begat a son
Lamech begetting a son, the future Noah
the line of promise continuing to Noah, the preserver of the line
the godly line reaching its goal in Noah

schools.summary: Lamech names Noah in hope of comfort: 'and he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed' — Lamech naming his son Noah (rest/comfort, from nuach 'to rest' / nacham 'to comfort'), declaring a hope that this son would bring comfort (the name expressing Lamech's longing for relief); Lamech hoping for comfort 'concerning our work and toil... because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed' — relief from the wearisome toil of cultivating the cursed ground (3:17-19), the hope that Noah would bring some easing of the curse and labor, fulfilled on one reading in Noah's agricultural discoveries (the cultivation of the vine, 9:20), or in the renewal of the earth after the flood (when the curse was, in a measure, eased, 8:21-22); Lamech's words looking back to the curse on the ground (3:17, the toil under the curse weighing on the godly, the hope of comfort and rest expressing the longing of fallen man for relief and, ultimately, for the Redeemer) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: What comfort did Lamech hope for through Noah?

sides.position: Relief from the toil of the cursed ground — through Noah's agriculture (the vine, 9:20) or the post-flood renewal of the earth (the literal reading)
A Messianic-tinged hope — a longing for the promised seed (3:15) who would bring true rest and comfort from the curse

themes: he called his name Noah, saying, This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands, because of the ground which the LORD hath cursed
Lamech naming Noah in hope of comfort
the hope of comfort and rest (relief from toil, or a Messianic-tinged longing)
the longing of fallen man for relief from the curse and the Redeemer

schools.summary: Lamech's long life after Noah: 'and Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters' — Lamech, like the other patriarchs, living to a great age (five hundred ninety-five years after Noah) and begetting many children (the long life and numerous offspring of the antediluvian fathers, though Lamech's total, 777 years, somewhat shorter than most); Lamech's long life part of the antediluvian pattern, though his total, v.31, less than the others (the patriarchs living for centuries, the long lives serving to people the earth and preserve the line of promise to Noah); 'begat sons and daughters' (Lamech having many other children besides Noah, the race multiplying through the patriarchs' offspring, the genealogy naming the line of promise, Noah, but recording the many sons and daughters, the increase of mankind before the flood) — Gill.

themes: Lamech lived after he begat Noah five hundred ninety and five years, and begat sons and daughters
Lamech's long life and many children after Noah
the great age of the patriarch and the multiplying of the race
the antediluvian pattern of longevity

schools.summary: The death of Lamech: 'and all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years: and he died' — Lamech's full life, seven hundred seventy-seven years (somewhat shorter than most of the patriarchs), and then the refrain, 'and he died,' the recurring toll of mortality (Lamech, the godly father of Noah, dying like the rest, before the flood); Lamech living seven hundred seventy-seven years, his life, though long, shorter than his father Methuselah's and others', yet, like all, ending in death (Lamech dying before the flood, which came after his death, in the year of Methuselah's death); 'and he died' the seal of mortality repeated over the last of the named patriarchs before Noah (the sentence of death, 3:19, taking effect, even the godly Lamech, who hoped for comfort through Noah, v.29, dying, the refrain closing the line of the patriarchs in death, death reigning over all the sons of Adam, Romans 5:14, save Enoch, the long lives ending in the grave, the chapter sealing the reign of death until the flood) — Gill.

themes: all the days of Lamech were seven hundred seventy and seven years: and he died
the total of Lamech's years and his death
the godly father of Noah dying before the flood
the refrain sealing the reign of death until the flood

schools.summary: Noah and his three sons: 'and Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth' — Noah, the tenth from Adam, the preserver of the line through the flood, begetting his sons at an advanced age (around five hundred years and after, Noah the goal of the genealogy, the bridge from the old world to the new, through him the race and the line of promise surviving the flood); 'Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth' (Noah's three sons, from whom the whole post-flood race would descend, 9:18-19; 10:1, the three sons named together though born over a period, through these three the earth repeopled after the flood, the line of promise continuing especially through Shem, in whose line would come Abraham and Christ); Shem, Ham, and Japheth the heads of the post-flood nations (ch. 10, the whole human race after the flood springing from them, the genealogy of ch. 5, having traced the line from Adam to Noah, ending at the threshold of the flood and the new world, the line of promise, preserved through Noah, continuing through Shem to Abraham and to Christ) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Noah was five hundred years old: and Noah begat Shem, Ham, and Japheth
Noah and the birth of his three sons
Noah the preserver of the line, the bridge from the old world to the new
the post-flood race springing from Shem, Ham, and Japheth
